label: Incident / status post
description: Outage or degradation comm. Studio drafts a status-page-shaped post using voice + product memory. One-shot, high-stress.
prior_art_examples:
Three real reference points shape this template:

1. Atlassian Statuspage uses a fixed four-state lifecycle: Investigating -> Identified -> Monitoring -> Resolved. Each state has a templated message body; the first post is typically '<system> is currently experiencing <symptom>. We are investigating.' followed by 'who is affected' and 'next update in N minutes'. Statuspage ships two example templates out of the box: 'Elevated Errors (Example)' and 'Processing Delays (Example)'. Atlassian's own canonical wording: 'We are investigating reports of <symptom> affecting <component>. We will provide an update within 30 minutes.' (https://support.atlassian.com/statuspage/docs/incident-template-library/, https://www.atlassian.com/incident-management/incident-communication/templates).

2. Better Stack's status page docs and 7-status-page-examples post emphasize: name the affected component, name the user-visible symptom (not the internal error), give an impact scope (all users / EU only / paid plans), commit to an update cadence rather than an ETA. Sample first-post shape from their templates: '<Component> degradation - increased latency and intermittent 5xx errors on the API. Customers in <region> may see <symptom>. Next update in <cadence>.' (https://betterstack.com/docs/uptime/creating-status-report-and-status-update/, https://betterstack.com/community/guides/incident-management/status-page-examples/).

3. Instatus and Hyperping template galleries lean harder on markdown body + a one-line headline + an explicit 'no ETA yet' line when an ETA cannot honestly be given. FireHydrant's guide ('Your guide to better incident status pages') is the canonical 'silence is worse than no-news' source: it's acceptable to post 'we are still investigating, no new information, next update in 30 minutes'. The shared idea: status posts are reassurance artifacts first, technical artifacts second; transparency about uncertainty beats false specificity (https://hyperping.com/blog/status-page-templates-for-incident-management, https://firehydrant.com/blog/your-guide-to-better-incident-status-pages/).

Representative output the studio should be able to draft (single-post v1):

  # Investigating: elevated error rates on the public API
  **Status:** Investigating
  **Started:** 2026-05-12 14:07 UTC
  **Components affected:** Public API (api.example.com), Webhook delivery
  **User impact:** A subset of API requests are returning 5xx errors. Webhook deliveries may be delayed. Dashboard sign-in is unaffected.
  **What we know:** Error rate began climbing at 14:07 UTC. Engineers are investigating <internal_dep>.
  **Next update:** by 14:45 UTC (or sooner if there's new information).
prompt_pattern:
Zero-shot with a strongly structured system prompt + heavy memory grounding. High-stress + low-frequency = no few-shot examples drawn from memory at runtime (the founder almost never has prior incidents in their brain on day 1; baking examples into the system prompt is fine).

System prompt skeleton (mirrors eng/adr-draft.ts):
  - Role line: 'You are drafting a public incident status post for a software product. Tone is calm, factual, and reassuring. Do not over-promise.'
  - voiceClause(brain.voice) -- inherit the marketing/voice register so the post sounds like the same product, just in a quieter key.
  - productClause(brain.product) -- so 'the public API' becomes the actual product name and audience.
  - vendorsClause(brain.vendors) -- only used to translate vendor-role into a user-facing component name (e.g. 'our email-delivery provider' rather than 'Postmark').
  - Task + first-use inputs (component, symptom, scope, start_time, current_status).
  - Output structure pinned with a code block scaffold (Status / Started / Components affected / User impact / What we know / Next update), exactly the shape adr-draft.ts uses.
  - Hard constraints (see safety_constraints).
  - ENG_CITATION_INSTRUCTION (cite product/vendor memories when they shape the wording, e.g. 'webhook delivery' is a glossary term).
  - OUTPUT_AS_PLAIN_MARKDOWN.

Brain memory is injected as a compact summary (BrainSummary shape already in templates/types.ts), not as raw markdown -- the studio pre-summarizes to keep tokens predictable in a one-shot, high-stress flow.
failure_modes:
1. Overpromising an ETA. Model is biased toward concrete numbers ('we expect this to be resolved within 30 minutes'). The prompt must forbid ETAs and force 'next update in N minutes' cadence instead.
2. Wrong tone for severity. Voice memory may say 'playful, lowercase, irreverent' (good for marketing) -- a 5xx incident post still needs to be calm and capitalized. Prompt must override voice's register-level rules for incident posts, while keeping vocabulary (product names, do/don't words).
3. Leaking internal terminology. Brain may contain 'Postmark', 'Cloudflare KV', internal service names. Output must translate to user-visible components only (cite vendors.md for the role -> user-facing-name mapping).
4. Apology that admits liability. 'We deeply apologize for the data loss our negligence caused' is bad legal posture for a young company. Prompt forces 'we know this is frustrating, here's what's happening, here's the next update' instead of liability-shaped language.
5. Premature resolution. If the user picks 'Identified' or 'Monitoring' but hasn't actually fixed anything, the model will pad the post with confident-sounding cause/fix language. Prompt must say 'only include a cause sentence if the user typed one; otherwise omit'.
6. Citing memory that doesn't exist. Standard hallucination risk -- mitigated by ENG_CITATION_INSTRUCTION's 'Never invent ids' rule already used by eng templates.
brain_memory_consumed:
- **voice** (vocabulary only, not register): pulls do_words / dont_words / glossary phrasing so the post sounds like the product. The register field is intentionally ignored or downshifted for incident posts -- a brand that's normally chatty still needs to write a calm incident post. The prompt explicitly overrides voice.register to 'calm, factual, capitalized, no emoji' regardless of brain.voice.register.
- **product** (positioning + target_user): tells the model 'who is affected' in plain user-facing language. positioning -> what to call the product; target_user -> how technical the audience is (developer-facing API vs. consumer app changes 'we hit a database hiccup' vs. 'API latency exceeded 2s').
- **glossary** (product terminology): so 'webhook' stays 'webhook' and 'workspaces' stays 'workspaces' rather than the model paraphrasing into 'callback endpoints' or 'tenants'. Treated as a hard do_words injection.
- **vendors** (role -> user-facing-name only): used in the rare case where the founder wants to disclose the upstream cause ('our email-delivery provider is experiencing an outage'). Never expose the vendor name unless the founder explicitly types it in the input.
- **team** (not consumed in v1): the post is signed as the product, not a person. Defer to v1.1 if founders want '- <FounderName>' signatures.
- **decisions** (not consumed in v1): incident posts are reactive, not strategic. v1.1 could pull 'we promised 99.9% uptime to enterprise' as a constraint, but that's polish.
- **source_artifact** (consumed for follow-up posts, not first post): if a prior incident post for the same component exists, surface its id so the founder can write a 'similar to last time' line. v1.1.
inputs_required:
firstUseInputs (mirrors eng-templates pattern):

1. component (required, kind: 'text', label: 'Affected component', hint: 'What's broken from the user's perspective? E.g. "public API", "sign-in", "webhook delivery". One line.')
2. symptom (required, kind: 'text', label: 'User-visible symptom', hint: '5xx errors, slow response, login loop, missing data. Plain language a customer would recognize.')
3. current_status (required, kind: 'select', options: ['investigating', 'identified', 'monitoring', 'resolved'], default: 'investigating', label: 'Current status', hint: 'Mirrors Statuspage's four states.')
4. impact_scope (optional, kind: 'text', label: 'Impact scope', hint: 'Who is affected? "All users", "EU customers only", "paid plans on the legacy stack". If unknown, leave blank and the post will say so.')
5. update_cadence (optional, kind: 'select', options: ['15 min', '30 min', '60 min'], default: '30 min', label: 'Next update in', hint: 'How often you'll post. Default is 30 min, matches industry norm.')
6. cause_summary (optional, kind: 'text', label: 'Cause (if known)', hint: 'Only fill this in if status is identified/monitoring/resolved AND you actually know. Leave blank otherwise -- the model will not invent one.')
output_block_kinds:
v1: single `plain` OutputBlock carrying markdown -- same as every engineering template. The markdown maps cleanly to most status-page tools' Markdown body fields (Statuspage, Better Stack, Instatus, Hyperping all accept markdown).

No new OutputBlock kind is warranted in v1. The post is one document; rendering it as a typed-preview card would add complexity without changing the founder's workflow (copy-paste into Statuspage or X).

v1.1 candidate: an `incident_post` typed kind with fields { status, title, body_markdown, components: string[], next_update_at?: string } -- useful only if we later integrate directly with a status-page provider (Statuspage / Better Stack API). Defer until that integration exists.
comparable_template_in_existing_studios: Closest analog: eng-templates/adr-draft.ts. Both are single-document, plain-markdown, structurally-scaffolded outputs with required inputs that map directly onto section headings (Context/Options -> Components/Symptom). Both ignore voice.register in favor of a domain-pinned tone. The buildPrompt shape, helper clauses (decisionsClause-style), and ENG_CITATION_INSTRUCTION + OUTPUT_AS_PLAIN_MARKDOWN reuse should be near-identical.
memory_writeback_proposal:
Two-stage writeback when the founder accepts a generated incident post:

1. **Always (cheap, automatic):** write a `source_artifact` memory_files row containing the final markdown, with frontmatter { kind: 'source_artifact', subkind: 'incident_post', component: <component>, status_at_post_time: <status>, started_at: <start_time>, owning_layer: 'manager' }. This is the audit trail -- past incident posts become searchable context for future ones ('the last time the API went down, here's what we said') and feed v1.1 follow-up-post drafting.

2. **Optional (one-click suggestion after Resolved post):** propose appending an entry to a `support/known-incidents.md` glossary memory listing component + symptom + duration + one-line cause. This is the BBC flywheel -- it converts a reactive event into reusable product knowledge. Surfaces under the same J.14/J.15 conversational flow as marketing: 'Want to remember this for next time? I'll add: "<component> has had <N> incidents this quarter, usually caused by <root_cause>."' Founder confirms; studio writes via the standard propose -> accept pipeline (no direct write, per Main rules).

Deliberately NOT writing back: voice changes, product changes, vendor changes. An incident post is too unusual to be evidence about voice or positioning -- writeback is scoped to the support/incident domain.
safety_constraints:
- NEVER commit to an ETA for resolution. Use 'next update in N minutes' cadence instead.
- NEVER write apologies that admit liability ('our negligence', 'we failed our customers', 'we caused data loss'). Use 'we know this is frustrating' / 'we're sorry for the disruption' framing.
- NEVER invent a cause. If cause_summary input is blank, the 'What we know' section says 'investigating' -- nothing more.
- NEVER name upstream vendors unless the founder explicitly typed the name in cause_summary. Even then, prefer the role ('our email-delivery provider') over the vendor name.
- NEVER leak internal service names, error messages, stack traces, or customer IDs even if they appear in the inputs.
- NEVER use emoji in incident posts (regardless of brand voice), and avoid exclamation points.
- NEVER promise compensation, refunds, or SLA credits in the post. Those are handled separately by the founder.
- NEVER use marketing-flavored superlatives ('lightning-fast', 'rock-solid'). Tone is factual.
- NEVER write more than ~120 words for the first 'Investigating' post. Conciseness is correctness here.
override_examples:
- 'we always sign incident posts with - The <ProductName> team' -> add_constraint (modifies the closing line of every post).
- 'never use the word "unfortunately"' -> forbid_pattern.
- 'for any auth-related incident, always include the line "You will not be signed out; existing sessions are unaffected"' -> add_constraint (scoped via input metadata).
- 'replace the standard "User impact:" header with "What you'll see:" -- we use second person on our status page' -> replace_section.
- 'when component=public API, always include a curl example showing how to retry' -> add_example.
- 'on a Resolved post, always end with "Full post-mortem coming within 48 hours."' -> add_constraint (status-conditional).
estimated_loc: ~160 LOC: ~30 lines of imports + Template metadata + firstUseInputs (six inputs is the longest input list across studios so far, vs. adr-draft's two), ~100 lines of buildPrompt (scaffold mirrors adr-draft.ts ~70 lines + status-conditional logic adds ~30), ~30 lines for a small helper clause that translates current_status into the per-section instructions. Slightly above the 120-200 LOC band's midpoint, anchored to adr-draft.ts as the closest existing template.
v1_essential: yes. Incident posts are exactly the one-shot-high-stress workflow Studios are designed for -- a founder at 2am with a half-broken product is the canonical reason to use BBC instead of a blank text box. Skipping this template in v1 would undercut the support-studio thesis. It's also the lowest-novelty template (closest analog already exists; reuses adr-draft.ts patterns), so it has the best cost/value ratio of the support templates.
